manifest.description: Blueprint-compiled draft dispatch pack for eng.grammar.comparatives-and-superlatives (Phase 1 front-end; DRAFT only)

assets.title: Comparatives and Superlatives — Conceptual Development Sequence
assets.content:
**TA-1 — Short Word Comparatives/Superlatives (-er/-est) [C]**

Present short adjectives (tall, fast, big — with attention to spelling changes: big→bigger, happy→happier); student produces comparative and superlative forms.

**TA-2 — Long Word Comparatives/Superlatives (more/most) [C]**

Present longer adjectives (beautiful, difficult, interesting); student produces comparative and superlative forms using "more/most," directly targeting MC-ALWAYS-ADD-ER-EST-OR-ALWAYS-USE-MORE-MOST.

**TA-3 — Irregular Comparatives/Superlatives [C→P]**

Present the fixed irregular set (good/better/best, bad/worse/worst, far/farther-further/farthest-furthest, little/less/least, much-many/more/most); student produces correct irregular forms, directly targeting MC-IRREGULAR-COMPARATIVES-ARE-JUST-MISTAKES.

**TA-4 — Using Comparatives/Superlatives in Context (Two vs. Three+ Items) [P]**

Present scenarios comparing exactly two items (requiring comparative + "than") vs. three or more items (requiring superlative + "the"); student selects and produces the correct form based on the number of items being compared.

---

assets.title: - **Trigger signal:** Student applies only one comparison strategy universally — either always adding "-er/-est" (producing errors like "beautifuler," "difficultest") or always using "more/most" (producing errors like "more tall," "most fast") — rather than applying the correct rule based on the adjective/adverb's SYLLABLE LENGTH (short words: -er/-est; longer words, typically 3+ syllables or certain 2-syllable patterns: more/most).
assets.content:
### MC-ALWAYS-ADD-ER-EST-OR-ALWAYS-USE-MORE-MOST
- **Trigger signal:** Student applies only one comparison strategy universally — either always adding "-er/-est" (producing errors like "beautifuler," "difficultest") or always using "more/most" (producing errors like "more tall," "most fast") — rather than applying the correct rule based on the adjective/adverb's SYLLABLE LENGTH (short words: -er/-est; longer words, typically 3+ syllables or certain 2-syllable patterns: more/most).
- **Conflict evidence [P28]:** "You said 'beautifuler.' Let's count the syllables in 'beautiful': beau-ti-ful — three syllables. Do longer, multi-syllable words like this usually take '-er,' or do they usually use 'more' instead?"
- **Bridge text [P30]:** "Short adjectives/adverbs (usually 1 syllable, and many common 2-syllable ones) take '-er/-est' (tall→taller→tallest, happy→happier→happiest). Longer adjectives/adverbs (usually 3+ syllables, and some 2-syllable ones ending in patterns like '-ful,' '-ous,' '-ing') take 'more/most' instead (beautiful→more beautiful→most beautiful). The choice depends on syllable length and specific word patterns, not personal preference or a single universal rule."
- **Replacement text [P31]:** "Check the syllable count and ending pattern of the adjective/adverb before choosing '-er/-est' or 'more/most' — don't apply one strategy to every word."
- **Discrimination pairs [P33]:**
  - "tall → taller → tallest" (short, 1 syllable, takes -er/-est) vs. "beautiful → more beautiful → most beautiful" (3 syllables, takes more/most).
  - "happy → happier → happiest" (2 syllables ending in -y, takes -er/-est with a spelling change) vs. "careful → more careful → most careful" (2 syllables ending in -ful, takes more/most).
- **s6_path:** "It's a reasonable instinct to want one consistent rule for all comparisons — but English genuinely splits this by word length/pattern, and checking syllable count is a reliable, learnable test."

assets.title: - **Trigger signal:** Student "corrects" genuinely irregular comparative/superlative forms (good→better→best, bad→worse→worst, far→farther/further→farthest/furthest) back to the regular pattern, producing errors like "gooder," "goodest," "badder," "baddest," not recognizing these as a small, fixed set of true exceptions that must be memorized rather than derived from the regular rule.
assets.content:
### MC-IRREGULAR-COMPARATIVES-ARE-JUST-MISTAKES
- **Trigger signal:** Student "corrects" genuinely irregular comparative/superlative forms (good→better→best, bad→worse→worst, far→farther/further→farthest/furthest) back to the regular pattern, producing errors like "gooder," "goodest," "badder," "baddest," not recognizing these as a small, fixed set of true exceptions that must be memorized rather than derived from the regular rule.
- **Conflict evidence [P28]:** "You said 'gooder' instead of 'better.' Is 'gooder' actually a word fluent English speakers use, or is there a special, different word used instead?"
- **Bridge text [P30]:** "A small set of very common adjectives/adverbs have IRREGULAR comparative and superlative forms that don't follow either the '-er/-est' or 'more/most' pattern: good→better→best, bad→worse→worst, far→farther/further→farthest/furthest, little→less→least, much/many→more→most. These must be memorized directly as a fixed, learnable list, since they can't be derived from the regular rules."
- **Replacement text [P31]:** "For a small set of very common words (good, bad, far, little, much/many), use the memorized irregular forms rather than applying the regular -er/-est or more/most rule."
- **Discrimination pairs [P33]:**
  - "good → better → best" (irregular, memorized) vs. attempting "gooder/goodest" (incorrect, regular pattern misapplied).
  - "bad → worse → worst" (irregular) vs. attempting "badder/baddest" (incorrect).
- **s6_path:** "Since regular rules work for the vast majority of words, it's very natural to expect them to apply everywhere — but a handful of extremely common, frequently-used words are genuine, learnable exceptions worth memorizing directly."

---

assets.title: Using Comparatives/Superlatives in Context (Two vs. Three+ Items)
MP-1
assets.content:
**MP-1 [P36] — Short word, novel example**
"Fill in: 'This box is ___ than that one' (using 'big')."
*Target:* "bigger" (with correct spelling — doubled consonant).

assets.title: MP-2
assets.content:
**MP-2 [P36] — Long word, novel example**
"Fill in: 'This is the ___ movie I've seen' (using 'exciting,' superlative for many movies compared)."
*Target:* "most exciting."

assets.title: MP-3
assets.content:
**MP-3 [P36] — Irregular form, novel example**
"Fill in: 'Of all the options, this is the ___' (using 'bad,' superlative)."
*Target:* "worst" — not "baddest."

assets.title: MP-4
assets.content:
**MP-4 [P36] — Two vs. three+ items**
"If comparing just two apples, do you say 'the reddest apple' or 'the redder apple'? What about comparing five apples?"
*Target:* two apples — "the redder apple" (comparative); five apples — "the reddest apple" (superlative).

assets.title: MP-5
assets.content:
**MP-5 [P36] — Explain the comparison system**
"In your own words: how do you decide between '-er/-est' and 'more/most,' and are there any words that don't follow either rule?"
*Target:* states or demonstrates that short words typically take -er/-est while longer words take more/most (based on syllable count/pattern), and that a small set of common words (good, bad, far, little, much/many) have irregular forms that must be memorized.

---

assets.title: Adaptive Flags
assets.content:
- **Syllable-count check is the reliable decision rule**: teach this explicitly as a concrete, checkable test rather than expecting intuition to develop unprompted.
- **Irregular forms are a small, fixed, memorizable list**: treat good/bad/far/little/much-many as a named exception set worth direct memorization, not a source of ongoing confusion to work around.
- **Comparative requires "than"; superlative requires "the"**: reinforce these companion-word patterns explicitly (TA-4) as part of correct usage, not just the adjective/adverb form itself.
- **Lower session_cap (4 TAs) reflects the concept's genuine scope**: comparatives-and-superlatives is a smaller, single-hour concept per the KG's `estimated_hours: 1` — the TA sequence is proportionally shorter, consistent with the established convention for concise concepts.

---

assets.title: Session Architecture
assets.content:
```
[P01] Session open — height-line comparison warm-up (two objects, then three)
  ↓
[P41] PD-1 (adjective readiness)
  ↓ PASS
[P41] PD-2 (adverb readiness)
  ↓ PASS
[P06] Anchor: height-line comparison (short-word -er/-est vs. long-word more/most)
  ↓
TA-1: Short word comparatives/superlatives (-er/-est) [C]
  ↓
TA-2: Long word comparatives/superlatives (more/most) [C]
  ↓
[P28] Conflict: one strategy applied universally → MC-ALWAYS-ADD-ER-EST-OR-ALWAYS-USE-MORE-MOST (if triggered)
  ↓
TA-3: Irregular comparatives/superlatives [C→P]
  ↓
[P28] Conflict: irregular forms "corrected" to regular pattern → MC-IRREGULAR-COMPARATIVES-ARE-JUST-MISTAKES (if triggered)
  ↓
TA-4: Using comparatives/superlatives in context (two vs. three+ items) [P]
  ↓
[P51] Check-in WE-1 → WE-2 → WE-3
  ↓
[P36] MP-1 through MP-5 (mastery probe set)
  ↓
[P62] Retrieval seed: "Tomorrow, compare two things and three things using the correct comparative and superlative forms."
[P68] Session close
[P85] Regulation tail — praise checking syllable length and irregular-list membership over guessing
[P89] Retrieval schedule: 1 day, 3 days, 7 days
[P91] Mastery gate: 75%; re-route to Component 3 anchor if below
```

**Protocol routing:** S0 (secure on adjectives/adverbs, new to comparison forms): dwell on TA-1's short-word pattern before introducing the more/most rule for longer words. S1 (already compares fluently in speech but errs on irregular forms in writing/formal contexts): focus on TA-3's irregular set, since these are common, persistent error sources even for fluent speakers. S6 (frustrated by needing to check syllable count before every comparison): validate that this is a quick, learnable check, and that with practice it becomes automatic. S9 (L1 has a single universal comparison strategy, e.g. many languages use one particle/word for all comparisons regardless of adjective length): expect the -er/-est vs. more/most split, and especially the irregular forms, to be a persistent transfer challenge requiring dedicated, explicit practice.

---
